content: Large language models (LLMs) generate text by predicting the most probable next token in a sequence, conditioned on the preceding context. This prediction relies on a vast, high-dimensional parameter space learned through training on extensive corpora using variants of the transformer architecture, which excels at capturing long-range dependencies via self-attention mechanisms. Self-attention computes contextualized token embeddings by dynamically weighting all tokens in the input sequence, enabling the model to contextualize each token’s representation relative to the entire sequence. These contextual embeddings are then passed through multi-layer feedforward networks and normalization layers, gradually transforming the input representation into a probability distribution over the vocabulary at each token position. Crucially, the model’s training objective—typically autoregressive maximum likelihood estimation—aligns parameter updates to maximize the likelihood of observed sequences, effectively teaching the model to emulate statistical co-occurrence patterns without explicit semantic understanding. At inference, generation proceeds token-by-token, sampling from the predicted distribution using strategies like greedy decoding or nucleus sampling, which balance coherence and diversity. Therefore, while LLMs do not possess true understanding or intent, their ability to model complex token interdependencies allows them to produce remarkably human-like text that aligns with learned probabilistic patterns. In sum, LLM text generation emerges from layering self-attention-based contextual embeddings with statistical learning and controlled sampling, yielding fluent but fundamentally probabilistic language outputs.
